turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Second case is Groff v. DeJoy. Under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, employers of sufficient size must accommodate their employees' religious practices unless doing so would result in an undue hardship. In this case, the Third Circuit found that the United States Postal Service had no duty to accommodate a postal worker who abstained from work on Sunday for religious reasons. Its ruling relied on the premise that anything more than a de minimis cost qualifies as an undue hardship. For reasons explained further in our opinion, we hold that this low standard misreads Title VII and our precedent and that an employer must accommodate its employees' religious practices unless accommodation would result in substantial increase caused in relation to the conduct of an employer's particular business. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurrence, which is joined by Justice Jackson.
